risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A stratified-block randomization method was used to assign all eligible persons to one of the intervention arms, stratified by BMI and gender." Comment: The study explicitly describes a randomization procedure with stratified blocks, which is a systematic random allocation method and provides sufficient detail to support truly random sequence generation.

Quote: "A stratified-block randomization method was used to assign all eligible persons to one of the intervention arms, stratified by BMI and gender." Comment: This describes the randomization approach/sequence structure, but does not report any allocation concealment mechanism. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, pharmacy-controlled assignment, or sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes. Therefore, whether the person enrolling participants could foresee the next assignment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "IDEA was a single-blind, single-center, 18-month, randomized controlled trial"; "Participants were randomized into one of the three groups: diet-induced weight loss only (D); diet-induced weight loss plus exercise (D + E); or exercise-only control (E)." Comment: The trial is explicitly only single-blind, and the interventions (diet program, supervised exercise, or both) are behaviorally distinct, so participants and intervention personnel would necessarily know group assignment. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence important study outcomes in this review context, especially self-reported pain, function, and quality of life, which the paper notes improved: "despite demonstrating improvements in pain and function" and "exercise also leads to improvement in self reported symptoms." Therefore lack of blinding is likely to affect outcomes susceptible to performance bias.

Quote: "IDEA was a single-blind, single-center, 18-month, randomized controlled trial" and "MRIs were read paired and unblinded to timepoint by a musculoskeletal radiologist with 14-year experience in semiquantitative MRI assessment and using the BLOKS method30." Comment: Outcome assessors included a musculoskeletal radiologist (clinician/imaging assessor) for semi-quantitative MRI outcomes, a single physician for alignment measures, and automated/software-based measurement for some radiographic outcomes. The trial was only single-blind, so participants were not fully blinded, and the paper notes that the broader IDEA trial demonstrated "significant improvements in pain and function," indicating subjective patient-reported outcomes were part of the trial outcome set. By the worst-outcome rule, unblinded participant self-report for subjective outcomes such as pain/function makes detection bias high overall, even though some imaging outcomes were objective or software-derived. In addition, MRI semi-quantitative scoring involved assessor judgement and was not blinded to timepoint.

Quote: "The IDEA study consisted of 454 ambulatory (a subsample of 325 persons had repeat X-rays and 105 had repeat MRIs for this analysis)..."; "All radiographs with paired acquisitions (baseline and FU18) are included in this analysis (n = 325)."; "MRI of the most symptomatic knee was performed on a sub-sample of study participants with 105 (of 454 participants) having both baseline and follow-up MRI scans acquired..."; and Table I footnote: "105 persons had baseline and 18 months MRIs of whom 98 had adequate quality images for the purposes of analysis." Comment: The paper gives overall analysed numbers for the structural outcomes at 18 months, but does not report post-randomization attrition per randomized arm for the main trial (152/152/150 randomized) nor reasons for missing outcome data by arm. For X-ray, Table II reports analysed n = 129, 136, 135, but the article does not explain losses/reasons by arm; for MRI, numbers are reported for a randomly selected subsample and image-quality exclusions, but this sub-design complicates separating intended subsampling from true post-randomization missingness. Because per-arm attrition/reasons on the analysed outcomes cannot be fully computed from the report, risk of attrition bias is unclear rather than high.
